candidate_problems.text: Does the 'public-goods underprovision of coherence' picture add explanatory content beyond standard decoherence, or is it only a redescription of environmental entanglement and practical inaccessibility? The key open problem is to formulate this analogy as a precise physical/information-theoretic criterion that yields new predictions, sharper distinctions between interpretations, or a principled account of when a subsystem should count as having a definite outcome.

What, if anything, turns branch-relative definiteness into unique actuality? The conjecture explicitly localizes the remainder of the measurement problem to whether there exists an ontologically privileged branch-selecting mechanism, but leaves open whether such a mechanism is needed at all. This raises a central unresolved tension between collapse-type theories, many-worlds, and epistemic/pragmatic readings of definiteness.

How should 'feasibility' or 'rational local availability' be defined in fundamental physics? The conjecture relies on coherence being globally present yet unrecoverable in practice, but it is unclear whether the relevant barrier is computational complexity, thermodynamic cost, causal locality, observer-relative access, or something else. A major open question is whether outcome definiteness can legitimately depend on such resource-bounded notions without making ontology observer-relative or merely FAPP.
